title: Cosalan / Benguet Province v. Lepanto Consolidated Mining Company (consolidated with G.R. 244216)
holding: Mining permits/agreements (EP/MPSA) are State-granted privileges, not vested property rights; the FPIC / NCIP Certification Precondition under IPRA is mandatory public policy that cannot be waived, and there is no vested right to renewal on old terms.

links.relevance: An MPSA is a revocable State privilege; FPIC/NCIP consent is a non-waivable precondition affecting the application and any consent dispute.
links.note: Consent/FPIC anchor for the MPSA application.

links.relevance: Permit rights are privileges subject to police power and FPIC; no vested renewal right.
links.note: Frames the permit as a conditional privilege.

title: Apex Mining Co. v. Southeast Mindanao Gold Mining Corp. (consolidated with G.R. 152628)
holding: An exploration permit confers only exploration rights and is a statutory privilege subject to strict conditions; transfer or assignment of an EP without the approval of the DENR Secretary is void.

links.relevance: Defines the nature/limits of an exploration permit and the DENR-approval requirement for any transfer.
EP rights are distinct from and preliminary to MPSA rights.

title: Garcia v. Esclito
holding: An action seeking to nullify deeds/documents which would as a consequence invalidate a Torrens title is a prohibited collateral attack; the title may be challenged only in a direct proceeding.

links.relevance: The estate-wide recovery must be a DIRECT nullity+reconveyance action, not an incidental attack, to cancel the derivative titles.
links.note: Governs the vehicle/forum for the estate-wide RTC action.

links.relevance: Explains why final title-cancellation cannot be obtained in the MTC summary case.

title: Rodriguez v. Court of Appeals
holding: A Torrens title cannot be altered or cancelled by collateral attack; conflicting title claims require a direct action for annulment/cancellation.

links.relevance: A direct action for annulment/cancellation is required to cancel the void-root derivative titles.
Reinforces that title validity is resolved in a direct proceeding.

title: Firaza, Sr. v. Ugay
holding: A counterclaim seeking annulment of title and reconveyance on the ground of fraud is a direct (not collateral) attack, which the defendant must be allowed to prove.

links.relevance: A defensive counterclaim to annul the Balane title on fraud/void-root grounds qualifies as a direct attack.
links.note: Enables raising nullity by counterclaim even in a case filed against us.

links.relevance: Counterclaim route to a direct attack.

title: Duenas v. Metropolitan Bank and Trust Company
holding: Under Art. 1544, the buyer who first registers in good faith has the better right; good faith must subsist from acquisition until registration, and awareness of a prior sale before registration destroys innocent-purchaser status.

links.relevance: Priority turns on good faith through registration; transferees with notice of the void root cannot prevail by registering first.
Registration tainted by bad faith is 'no registration at all'.

title: Gonzales v. Basas
holding: Prior registration alone does not confer a better right under Art. 1544; registration must be coupled with good faith, and knowledge of a prior claim before registration taints it.

links.relevance: Registration must be in good faith; knowledge of the prior/valid claim defeats the later registrant.
First-to-register does not win if in bad faith.

title: Requina, Sr. v. Erasmo
holding: The presumption of regularity of a notarized deed is disputable; upon clear evidence of irregular notarization/execution, the instrument may be reduced to a private document whose due execution and authenticity must be separately proved.

links.relevance: The notarization of the de la Fuente instruments does not immunize them; the presumption is rebuttable.
links.note: Opens the door to attacking the notarized void deeds.

links.relevance: Notarized deed is only prima facie; reducible to a private document.

title: Trazona v. Cañada
holding: Clear and convincing proof of forgery (credible testimonial and/or physical evidence) overcomes the presumption of regularity of a notarized deed.

links.relevance: Sets the clear-and-convincing quantum to prove the deeds are forged/void.
Quantum of proof to defeat the notarized deed.

title: Tortona v. Gregorio
holding: Expert and physical evidence showing that a signature or thumbmark is forged rebuts the presumption of regularity and renders the deed void.

links.relevance: Expert/physical signature evidence can void the de la Fuente conveyances.
Forged signature/thumbmark voids the deed.
